segments.s591-g57: سپردند is a past transitive verb (3rd pl.) 'they entrusted/handed over' and comes first; گردان and تاج و گاه are its direct objects (troops; 'crown and throne/seat' — two coordinated nouns joined by و). بدو is the indirect object 'to him' (بد + او fused), placed between object and the coordinated nouns. Note that گاه is a poetic/archaic word that here functions as 'throne/seat' (hence 'crown and throne'), though some lexicons list its basic sense as 'time'.
segments.s591-g58: برو here is used as a discourse/temporal particle 'thereupon/then' (literally an imperative 'go' used adverbially). انجمن شد is a simple past (انجمن 'assembly' + شد 'became/was formed'), meaning 'an assembly was convened'. ز هر سو سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'an army/from every side' giving the source/appearance; word order places that phrase after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s591-g25: This is a nominal clause with the predicate noun followed by the copula. سرافراز is an epithet (adjective/noun) placed before the proper name; بهرام is the name of the person; فرزند او is a possessive nominal phrase 'his son' (فرزند = 'son' and او = 'his/her'); ست is the archaic/poetic form of the copula (equivalent to است) attaching to فرزند او to make 'is his son.' Word order: [epithet] [name] [predicate + copula].
segments.s591-g26: ز introduces the means or sphere ('by/in/with'); the three nouns مغز، دل، رای are a coordinated list giving intellectual, emotional and consultative faculties. پیوند is the predicate noun 'bond/link' and اوست (او + ست) is the possessive + copula construction meaning 'is his/it is his' (here: 'he is bound to him' or 'he is his bond'). In effect the phrase says he is linked to/associated with him by mind, heart, and counsel.
segments.s591-g27: ز here is a poetic/discourse particle ('then' or 'from') introducing the speaker; منذر is a personal name. گشاید is the classical/present-form of گشودن used with سخن (گشودنِ سخن) meaning 'to open/begin speech' — here 'begins to speak.' سخن is the object 'speech' and سربسر is an adverbial 'throughout/at length' modifying گشاید.
segments.s591-g28: نخواهیم is first-person plural negative (we will not / we do not wish); it heads reported speech. بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'on the throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne) and بیدادگر is a postpositive adjective 'tyrannical/tyrant' modifying تخت. Together they state the content of the speech: 'We will not [sit] on a tyrant's throne.'
segments.s591-g29: سوگندهای گران is the object phrase 'heavy/weighty oaths' (گران = 'heavy/solemn'). بخوردند uses the idiom سوگند خوردن ('to take an oath') where خوردن ('to eat') is used idiomatically; here بخوردند is past-plural 'they took/swore.'
segments.s591-g30: هرانکس که بودند ایرانیان is a relative/free-choice construction meaning 'whoever the Iranians were' or more idiomatically 'the Iranians, whoever they were.' که is the relative/linking particle and بودند is the past plural of بودن ('they were'). The whole segment identifies the agents who took the oaths.
segments.s591-g31: کزین تخمه = 'from this seed/stock/lineage' (کزین = 'from this'); کس را is a fronted indefinite object marked by را ('anyone' as the object). به شاهنشهی is the prepositional phrase 'to the kingship/imperial rule' (به = to/for, شاهنشهی = imperial rule). Grammatically this is the object phrase that the following negative verb will deny: 'we will not make anyone of this line king.'
segments.s591-g32: نخواهیم is the first-person plural negative ('we will not' / 'we do not wish') and takes the preceding object phrase. با تاج و تخت مهی is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with crown and moon‑like (splendid) throne' (با = with; تاج = crown; تخت = throne; مهی = 'moon‑like' / 'beautiful' used as an epithet). Together they complete the refusal: 'we will not (raise) anyone to the kingship with crown and splendid throne.'
segments.s591-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. در دخمه is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/inside the tomb'); شد here functions as a past-state verb ('was placed/was come to be') with the predicate before the subject, and شهریار جهان is the noun phrase 'the sovereign/king of the world' (شهریار 'king' with جهان as a title/appositive). Word order (چو + locative + شد + subject) is typical of older/epic Persian where the copular verb follows the place phrase.
segments.s591-g2: ز ایران برفتند is a simple past clause: ز ایران 'from Iran' (preposition + place) + برفتند 'they departed/went away' (3rd person plural past). گریان مهان is a participial adjective phrase qualifying مهان; here گریان ('weeping') qualifies مهان ('guests, notables') with a poetic inversion (adjectival/participial element preceding or tightly attached to the noun).
segments.s591-g3: This is a coordinated nominal group listing people: کنارنگ (a proper name or title) + با 'with' + موبد 'the mobed (Zoroastrian priest)' + و 'and' + پهلوان 'the champion/hero'. The sequence is a pre-verbal list of actors; note کنارنگ is a name/title and therefore not interpreted as the literal lexical sense 'beside/marginal'.
segments.s591-g4: هشیوار here works as an epithet/adjective ('alert, clear‑headed') likely attaching to the preceding پهلوان. دستور روشن‌روان is a possessive/genitive-type phrase where دستور 'the order/command' is followed by روشن‌روان (the name or epithet 'Roshan‑rovan'); Persian poetic style often places the possessor after the possessed without an explicit ezāfe in older registers.
segments.s591-g5: همه پاک is a nominal phrase 'all (the) pure ones' — پاک functions as an epithet (honorific/adjectival). در پارس is the locative 'in Pars (Persia)'. گرد آمدند (گرد + آمدند) is a common compound in older Persian meaning 'gathered' or 'came together' (گرد as an adverbial/collective element + آمدند 'they came').
segments.s591-g6: بر دخمه یزدگرد آمدند: بر with آمدند expresses motion toward/onto a place here ('came to / upon the tomb'); دخمه یزدگرد is 'Yazdegerd's tomb' (possessive relationship is expressed by placing the owner's name after the place, as is common in epic Persian). The verb آمدند repeats to mark the action 'they came'.
segments.s591-g7: چو is the temporal/comparative 'as/when'. گستهم is a proper name (the epic hero Gostaham) rather than the lexical verb sense listed in the glosses. کو is an archaic relative/locative particle here (introducing a descriptive/relative clause) and does not correspond exactly to the provided glosses for 'where'/'where is'. پیل کشتی بر اسپ is an elliptical, highly compressed epic phrase: پیل 'elephant' and کشتی 'ship' and بر اسپ 'on a steed' are juxtaposed for vivid image; the main verb is implied by context. In short, this segment strings names and pictorial nouns together in an archaic/poetic way rather than a fully analytic clause.
segments.s591-g8: دگر = 'another/other'; قارن گرد appears as a (compound) proper name here (so the lexical senses 'companion/comparer' do not apply). پور گشسپ is a formulaic patronymic: پور 'son (of)' + گشسپ 'Goshasp' (the father's name). The sequence is appositional: 'another (was) Qaren‑Gard, son of Goshasp' with the copula omitted, typical of epic Persian listing.
segments.s591-g49: بجویید is the imperative/optative plural (or polite command) of جستن — 'seek' / 'look for'. بخشنده‌ای is a noun with the indefinite suffix -ای = 'a generous one' (object of the verb). دادگر is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'just' (literally 'justice‑doing'), so the whole phrase = 'Seek a generous, just man.'
segments.s591-g50: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this clause to the person sought. بندد is the classical 3rd‑person-present/optative form of بستن (here meaning 'to bind' or idiomatically 'to gird [one's loins]'); it expresses what the sought man will do. بر + ین = بر این 'on/for this' (ین is the orthographic variant of این). تخت زرین is a noun phrase 'golden throne' (تخت 'throne' modified by زرین 'golden'). کمر here is the object of بندد in the idiom کمر بستن 'to gird (one's) loins' — so the clause means 'who will gird his loins for this golden throne' (i.e. take up the throne).
segments.s591-g51: که again is a relative 'who/that'. آشوب is a noun 'turmoil/strife'. بنشاند is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person form of نشاندن (here transitive: 'cause to sit/settle' → 'to settle/quiet'); so آشوب بنشاند از روزگار = 'who will settle/put an end to the turmoil of the times.' از روزگار literally 'from/of the age' functions as the domain ('of the age/times').
segments.s591-g52: This is a nominal sentence with the copula -یست ('is'). جهان = 'the world' or 'this life'. مرغزاریست is the predicate 'is a مرغزار' (a meadow or bird‑haunt); the suffix -یست is the copula. بی‌شهریار is a prefixed adjective 'without a shah/sovereign' (بی‑ = without, شهریار = king), so the second clause says 'the world is a birds' meadow without a king' (i.e. prey to disorder).
segments.s591-g53: یکی = 'a certain' (introducing someone). مرد = 'man' (the noun head). The sequence بد پیر خسرو is poetic compression: بد here is an archaic/poetic form (in classical Persian poetry often contracted from بود) and functions with پیر خسرو to situate him as an elder or veteran associated with Khosrow; the phrase is best read as a string of appositive descriptors followed by به نام 'by the name'. به نام introduces the personal name that follows. (In short: 'There was a certain man, an old man of Khosrow's court, called...').
segments.s591-g54: This segment gives the man's name and epithets. جوانمرد is presented as his name (and also means 'chivalrous man'). The coordinating و 'and' links the following adjectives: روشن‌دل 'clear‑hearted / bright‑souled' and شادکام 'fortunate / joyful'. Grammatically this is a simple list of names/qualities describing the man.
segments.s591-g55: هم = 'also' / 'too'. از تخمه سرفرازان literally 'from the seed/stock of the sarfrazan' — تخمه here means 'offspring/lineage' and سرفرازان is plural 'the proud/honored (ones)'. بد اوی is an archaic poetic construction: بد is a contracted past copula (cf. بود) and اوی is the old 3rd‑person pronoun 'he', so together 'he was'. The whole means 'He too was of the noble stock.'
segments.s591-g56: به مرز اندر = 'in/within the border' (به + مرز + اندر, a locative phrase). از بی‌نیازان = 'from/out of the self‑sufficient (ones)' i.e. 'counted among the well‑provided/independent.' Again بد اوی repeats the archaic past copula + pronoun 'he was.' So this clause means 'and within the realm he was among the self‑sufficient.'
segments.s591-g17: جهاندارمان is a compound: جهاندار (world-ruler, sovereign) + ـمان (our) = “our sovereign.” تا جهان آفرید is a temporal clause (تا = ‘since/until’) with جهان (‘the world’) as object and آفرید (created) as past verb; together it means “since/when our sovereign created the world.”
segments.s591-g18: کسی ... ندید is a negative indefinite pattern meaning “no one saw/has seen.” زین here functions as the classical contraction of ز + این = “of/from this” (introducing the following noun phrase). نشان شهریاری is a genitive phrase “a mark/token of kingship,” with نشان (token/sign) modified by شهریاری (kingship, sovereignty). ندید is the past negative of دید (“to see”).
segments.s591-g19: که is the relative/linking particle tying this clause to the previous clause (“who/that”). جز means “except/other than” and introduces the only things listed: کشتن, خواری, درد, رنج — these are verbal-nominal forms (infinitive-as-nouns or abstract nouns) listing the harmful outcomes: “killing, humiliation, pain and suffering.”
segments.s591-g20: بیاگندن is an older/poetic verbal form meaning “to bring/take/gather” (here describing what such people do). از is the preposition “from,” چیز is a general noun “things/possessions,” درویش means “the poor/dervish” (here ‘the poor’), and گنج is “treasure.” The phrase literally says they ‘take/gather treasure from the poor’s things’ — i.e. they strip the poor of whatever treasure they have.
segments.s591-g21: ازین is from + این (here used in a comparative sense “than this/than he”), followed by شاه (king). ناپاک‌تر is the comparative form of ناپاک (“impure/immoral”), formed with the comparative suffix ـ‌تر. کس ندید is the negative-indefinite pattern “no one saw/has seen,” so the clause reads “no one has seen a king fouler/meaner than this one.”
segments.s591-g22: نه is the coordinating negative “nor,” here linking a second negative clause. از نامداران پیشین is a genitive/partitive phrase “from/of the renowned ones of old” (نامداران = renowned/illustrious people, پیشین = former/earlier). شنید is the past of شنیدن (“to hear”); together: “nor has one heard (of such) among the famed of old.”
segments.s591-g23: نخواهیم is first-person plural negative of خواستن and here reads as a firm refusal “we will not / we do not want.” بر تخت = “on the throne.” زین تخمه‌کس: again زین is the contracted z + in (= “of this”), modifying تخمه‌کس (lit. “seed-person” = scion/one of this stock); the whole phrase means “we will not have any scion of this stock on the throne.”
segments.s591-g24: ز (poetic form of از) = “from.” خاکش (“his dust”) is literal but used idiomatically for his lowliness or origin; به یزدان پناهیم is a standard supplicatory formula “we take refuge in God” with پناهیم first-person-plural present/subjunctive, and و بس = “and that's enough / and no more.” The clause expresses seeking divine protection from the man’s baseness and closing the matter.
segments.s591-g9: چو is the comparative/temporal particle 'like/as'. میلاد and پارس are proper names listed in coordination with و; مرزبان follows پارس as a title (Pars the marzbān). Word order is parallel: 'چو X و چون Y Z' = 'like X and like Y the Z.'
segments.s591-g10: Again چو introduces a comparison 'like'. پیروز here qualifies the following epithet اسپ‌افگن ('horse‑breaker'); از means 'from/among' (i.e. 'one of') and links to گرزبان, a title 'mace‑bearer'. The phrase reads 'like Pīrūz, the horse‑breaker, one among the mace‑bearers.'
segments.s591-g11: دگر 'the other/the rest' starts the clause; هرک 'whoever' is an indefinite subject; بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' and مهان is the predicative noun ('mehan', here 'guests/nobles').
segments.s591-g12: بزرگان و کنداوران is a coordinated noun phrase 'great ones and kanda‑varān'; جهان follows as a genitive/attributive 'of the world', modifying the whole coordinated phrase (i.e. 'great ones of the world and excavators of the world').
segments.s591-g13: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. خوارشان is خوار + شان ('their despised ones' or 'those you treated as low'); داشتی is second‑person past 'you had/kept'; یزدگرد is the proper name of the king, here in address or as subject. The clause reads: 'Where did you, Yazdegerd, keep/treat them as contemptible?'
segments.s591-g14: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. اندرَان (اندر + ان) here means 'into/therein' ('into that place' or 'therein'), and شهرگرد indicates the location sense 'in/around the city' (an adverbial/place expression). Together: 'All came into that city.'
segments.s591-g15: چنین گفت is the formulaic 'thus said'. گویا here is the parenthetical adverb 'it seems/it appears' that qualifies the report. گشسپ دبیر is the nominative proper name plus title 'Goshasp the scribe' serving as the speaker (verb before subject is common in epic register).
segments.s591-g16: که‌ای is a vocative/address particle here ('O' or 'you who are'); نامداران is the addressee 'renowned ones'. برنا 'young' and پیر 'old' are coordinated adjectives (with و 'and'), so the line addresses both the young and the old among the famed.
segments.s591-g41: جهانی here functions as a noun phrase 'the world/the whole world' placed before the adjective — a poetic word order. پرآشوب is a compound adjective meaning 'tumultuous/uproarious'. شد is the past tense of شدن 'to become' so پرآشوب شد = 'became tumultuous'. سر به سر is an adverbial idiom ('all over/throughout'); it follows the verb and modifies the whole clause.
segments.s591-g42: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از تخت گم شد is a past event: از (from/out of) + تخت 'throne' + گم 'lost/removed' + شد 'became/was' — together 'when (the head) was lost from the throne'. سر is the noun 'head' (literal/figurative). تاجور here refers to the crown‑bearer/crowned one (an archaic/poetic label); the word list given did not include that sense, so it is treated as the crown‑bearer.
segments.s591-g43: به ایران sets the location ('to/into Iran' or 'in Iran'); the preposition appears before the place. The author lists social types: رد (an older title — not matched to the provided senses), و 'and', موبد 'mōbed, Zoroastrian priest', و and پهلوان 'champion/hero'. The three nouns are coordinated in a catalogue style.
segments.s591-g44: هرانکس که بودند is a relative clause: هرانکس 'whoever' + که 'that/who' + بودند past plural of بودن 'they were' — 'whoever of them were'. روشن‌روان is a descriptive compound 'bright‑soul/clear‑minded' modifying those people. The clause qualifies the previously listed groups.
segments.s591-g45: بدین کار is an adverbial phrase 'for this purpose/with this matter' (بدین = به + این). در پارس 'in Pars/Persia' gives the place. گرد آمدند is a compound verb meaning 'they came together/they assembled' (گرد + آمدن), with آمدند the past plural 'they came'.
segments.s591-g46: بسی here is an intensive quantifier 'many/much' (poetic use). زین is the prepositional compound 'از + این' meaning 'from these' and links to نشان 'signs/tokens'. داستانها 'stories/tales' is the object, and زدند (here 'زدند' in the sense 'to perform/tell' or 'to strike out' in poetic usage) means 'they related/told' — so 'from these signs they spun many stories.'
segments.s591-g47: که here introduces the interrogative content 'who' (roughly 'who is it that...'). این تاج شاهی is a noun phrase 'this crown of kingship/this royal crown' (تاج 'crown' + شاهی 'kingship/royal'). سزاوار 'deserving/fit' is a predicative adjective and کیست 'who is (it)' completes the question in the archaic word order 'سزاوار کیست' = 'who is deserving'.
segments.s591-g48: ببینید is an imperative/plural 'look/see/find out'. تا here functions like 'so that' / 'in order that' introducing the following clause; combined with ببینید it reads 'see/let us see'. از در کار literally 'from the door/house/line of the affair' — idiomatically 'of what house/line/family he is' — and کیست closes with 'who is it'. Overall: 'Find out of what house/line he is.'
segments.s591-g33: برین is بر + the enclitic/orthographic -ین (here functioning like 'this' = 'on this/thereon'), so the phrase means 'on this' or 'thereon'. برنهادند is a classical past (3rd pl.) of نهادن with the prefix بر- meaning 'they set up/raised (onto it)'. و connects a second past verb, برخاستند (3rd pl. past of برخاستن) 'they rose/stood up'. The two verbs are coordinated actions by the same subject.
segments.s591-g34: همی is a narrative/intonation particle (here with a sense like 'so/thus' or emphatic 'indeed'), introducing the following statement. شهریاری is an abstract noun 'kingship/sovereignty' and دگر 'another' modifies it. خواستند is the 3rd person plural past of خواستن 'they wanted/sought', taking شهریاری دگر as its object.
segments.s591-g35: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی (literally 'awareness/information') governs the genitive chain 'آگاهیِ مرگِ شاهِ جهان' = 'the news/information of the death of the king of the world' (successive genitive construction: news of the death of the king). This whole noun phrase is the subject of the following predicate.
segments.s591-g36: پراگنده is a predicative/adjectival verbal phrase meaning 'spread/widespread' and is combined with the past auxiliary/verb شد 'became' to give an intransitive result 'became widespread / spread'. در میان مهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'among the guests' (در + میان used together for emphasis on 'in the midst of').
segments.s591-g37: الان is the adverb 'now' setting the time. شاه is the noun 'the shah/king'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the shah to the next appositional or coordinated phrase. چون here introduces the following noun phrase and can be read as 'when/as'; پارس پهلوسپاه is a compound apposition meaning 'the Pars (Persian) flank-commander' (پهلوسپاه = commander of the army's flank).
segments.s591-g38: چو (again) is temporal 'when'. بیورد is a classical 3rd-person past form (from آوردن) meaning 'he brought' or 'he came with/bringing'. و connects the following noun group شگنان زرین کلاه: شگنان (a plural noun) here denotes 'destroyers' or aggressive persons, and زرین کلاه is a modifier-noun phrase 'golden helmets' (i.e. 'golden-helmeted [men]'). Grammatically this whole clause describes an arrival/bringing by the flank-commander accompanied by a group described by their golden helmets.
segments.s591-g39: همی here is the iterative/progressive narrative particle (rendered 'kept'/'continued to'). هریکی is 'each one' (every man). گفت is the simple past 'said' (speech verb); شاهی is the predicate noun 'kingship' (شاهی = kingship), and مراست is an archaic fused copular-possessive 'مرا است' = 'is mine' (here written as one word). The clause is a direct reported saying: 'Each one said [that] the kingship is mine.'
segments.s591-g40: هم از ... تا is a correlative pair giving a range 'even/from ... up to ...'. از خاک 'from the earth/dust' and تا برج ماهی 'to the tower of the moon' form a poetically hyperbolic span (from low to high). مراست repeats the archaic 'مرا است' = 'it belongs to me' (copula + possessive). The whole clause emphasizes that each claimant declares the kingship theirs over the entire realm, 'from dust to the moon's tower.'

sentences.s591-s29: They entrusted to him the troops, the crown and the throne; thereupon an assembly was convened and an army came from every side.
sentences.s591-s13: Proud Bahram is his son, and in mind, heart, and counsel he is bound to him.
sentences.s591-s14: Munẓir began to speak at length; 'We will not sit upon the throne of a tyrant.'
sentences.s591-s15: The Iranians — whoever they were — took solemn oaths.
sentences.s591-s16: From this stock we will not raise anyone to the imperial kingship with crown and a moon‑like throne.
sentences.s591-s1: When the sovereign of the world was laid in the tomb, guests from Iran departed weeping.
sentences.s591-s2: Kanarang, together with the mobed and the champion Hoshiwaar, obeyed Roshan‑rovan's command.
sentences.s591-s3: All the pure gathered in Pars, and they came to Yazdegerd's tomb.
sentences.s591-s4: As for Gostaham — where an elephant bore a ship on a steed — and another (was) Qaren‑Gard, son of Goshasp.
sentences.s591-s25: Seek a generous, just man who will gird his loins for this golden throne.
sentences.s591-s26: who will quell the turmoil of the age — the world is a meadow for birds without a sovereign.
sentences.s591-s27: There was a certain man, an elder of Khosrow’s retinue, named Javanmard — clear‑hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s591-s28: He too was of the noble stock, and within the border he was among the self‑sufficient.
sentences.s591-s9: Since our lord created the world, no one has seen such a mark of kingship.
sentences.s591-s10: who, apart from killing, humiliation, pain and suffering, take from the poor’s possessions nothing but treasure.
sentences.s591-s11: No one has seen a king more impure than this one, nor has one heard of such among the famed men of old.
sentences.s591-s12: We will not (permit) anyone of this stock on the throne; we seek refuge in God from his dust — and that is enough.
sentences.s591-s5: Like Milād and like Pārs the marzbān, like Pīrūz the horse‑breaker among the mace‑bearers.
sentences.s591-s6: The others — whoever they were — were from Iran, the mehan: great men and excavators of the world.
sentences.s591-s7: Where, Yazdegerd, did you hold them in contempt? All of them came into that city.
sentences.s591-s8: Thus said, it seemed, Goshasp the scribe: 'O renowned ones, young and old!'
sentences.s591-s21: The whole world was thrown into turmoil everywhere, when the crowned head was lost from the throne.
sentences.s591-s22: In Iran, nobles, mobeds (Zoroastrian priests) and champions — whoever of them were bright‑souled.
sentences.s591-s23: For this purpose they assembled in Pars, and from these tokens they told many tales.
sentences.s591-s24: Who is deserving of this royal crown? Find out — see of what house/line he is.
sentences.s591-s17: They lifted him thereon and stood up; they demanded another kingship.
sentences.s591-s18: When news of the death of the king of the world spread among the guests,
sentences.s591-s19: Now the shah — and when the Pars flank-commander came and brought (news), along with the golden-helmeted destroyers —
sentences.s591-s20: Each of them kept saying, 'The kingship is mine' — even from the earth up to the moon's tower it belongs to me.
